speaker: interviewer
text: Two. Mrs. Heilman. You were discussing Jewish decrees. Can you elaborate on what else was involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I mentioned before, we had to give away our furs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember the sequence exactly, but what happened was the-- the Jews were not allowed to be on the street after certain hours. The Jews were obliged to wear white armbands with a blue Star of David, Magen David, on the left arm. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you? Who gave you the arm band?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very interesting because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the situation was awkward in terms of living. The Jews couldn't-- they were fired from their position in non-Jewish or public offices. So-- all there was is scramble to make a living. And it's interesting that-- that there was the whole industry developed about the armbands. My mother made us the white armbands, and she embroidered the light blue Magen David on it. But there was a whole industry when people started to manufacture wholesale armbands made out of celluloid, if you know what celluloid was. Celluloid is precursor of plastic. Those were permanent in the sense that you can just wipe it off. You didn't have to wash them and iron them. They were made out of paper in case you forgot yours, so you could buy it on the street made out of paper. It was a whole industry about the armband.

speaker: interviewer
text: To me, at that time I was then-- what? 12, 13. It was a badge of honor. I didn't recognize the intention of demeaning-- demeaning Jews as such. I didn't mind having it. I was proud wearing it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, the movement between the Jewish and non-Jewish parts was still very free. So when I used to go with Isabela, I used to take it off. Simply because we are not allowed to go to the non-Jewish parts. And at that time, I remember that the Polish factory, the chocolate factory, Wedel opened branches, and there were queues. And we used to go with Isabela to buy chocolates, and this was a big adventure. I also remember that there were probably kitchens opened. And one day, Isabela took me to a public kitchen when there was a soup. And we were having the soup, which was wonderful. Those were the little vignettes. But the restriction against the Jews was, as I mentioned, you know, the lack of employment, the [INAUDIBLE]. There were many people resettled from other places, coming into the Jewish section.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, it was confusion and fright. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But at that time, there was-- they were also starting-- the sequence is a little vague in my mind, but the ghetto started. And people were coming from outside towns into the Warsaw ghetto. And there were all kinds of rumors, and the negative rumors were dismissed. And the Jews in Warsaw felt that if all the Jews are being brought into Warsaw, this is with the intention of saving the Jews, just keeping them in one place. And this kind of hopeful thinking persisted until and even after the walls of the ghetto started to be built and the ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was in 1941. At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the teachers who could not teach outside of the ghetto organized themselves in the ghetto, and they created an educational system. Saying that after the war, those certificates of education would be recognized as bona fide. So we used to-- I used to go and attend classes in the regular schools that were taking place in different teachers' apartments. My sister, in the meantime, joined the school of nursing. And since my father couldn't work anymore, and everybody had to register themselves in some kind of a profession, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father registered as a maker of wooden frames. And as such, he got a license to open and supply the Germans with wooden photograph frames. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have to tell you that at that time, there were already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] transport of Jews from Warsaw into unknown places. And only those that were quote, 'employed,' could stay. If you didn't have some kind of a piece of paper testifying that you are employed, you were taken. And the first transport that were taken were the people-- were older people, sick people who came from outside of Warsaw. Those were the first transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of you have any idea where they were being transported to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember it must have been in 1940, '41. At that time, I joined the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Polish Youth-- Jewish Polish youth group, Hashomer Hatzair. And in 1940 or '41, two young men from Vilna, which is east Poland, came and we had a big, big conference in the big synagogue of Warsaw on Tlomackie street. These two young men, one of them I believe was Yehuda Wengrower. And the other one, I don't remember. They were telling us about the atrocities that the Germans were perpetrating upon the Jews, about the mass killing of the Jews in the Vilna ghetto. And I remember that at the end of the conference when we all stood up to thank the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], it was so strong that the walls shook. Then I came home and I told my father about it, and my father dismissed it completely. And said to me, don't say anything. Don't create panic of these warmongers. And don't disturb your mother. So whether he knew and tried to deny it, I couldn't tell you. And whether my mother knew, I couldn't tell you. But we knew. And I think that this was the beginning of the seed of the Warsaw ghetto rebellion, this particular meeting. I continue going to school. I remember also it must have been in 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the allied forces entered the war, there was a blackout. And in Warsaw ghetto, it was all dark. And people were walking around on the street with phosphorous little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pins so that they could see each other walking. Otherwise, you couldn't see anything. In Warsaw ghetto was at that time a terrible, terrible hunger. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was-- it was indescribable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no food coming in. There was no food in the ghetto. The rations that were received were not sufficient for anything. It was enough to have two pieces of bread per day, which you had supplement with potatoes if you could get it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were children whose parents were transported out, orphans on the streets. And you could see them from day to day sitting on the streets, getting thinner and thinner, and then suddenly getting bigger. When they were in the last stage of the hunger, they were-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't-- they were bloated. And they used to die on the street. I remember one day when my sister and I were walking on the street, and she bought some detergent for washing. And the young-- and we were walking through. This was wrapped up. And the young man fell on her and sunk his teeth right into it, because what happened was he thought it was food. And what was happening is that the Jewish police was running after the youngsters that were assaulting people, and ripping their possessions off their hands, and confiscating it. So they have learned when they took something, immediately put it in their mouth so that it won't be taken away from them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Also the transports were getting more and more often. We used to have my aunt, my father's sister living with us. And like many other people, because they didn't know what tomorrow will bring, people were walking around with knapsacks where they kept all of their most precious possessions in case they're going to be taken away. At least they will have it with them. And she was taken with her knapsack one day off the street, and we never saw her again. At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was telling you that my father was registered in making this wooden frame. And he got an apartment which was vacated, an empty apartment to have his workshop in. And my father told my sister and me to start taking the wooden frames and put it there is to make it look occupied. And one day, my sister and I were walking. It was just across the street from where we were, and there was a selection. Selection means-- not a selection. It was a run. What it meant was that the streets were cordoned off by the SS, the Polish police, and the Jewish police. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the Germans were running on the streets and in the buildings telling people to get out. No papers would help you at that point. Everybody out, out, out. My sister and my mother was in our apartment. My sister and I were in this empty apartment. And we started to run into a bunker, which was a hiding place where people used to hide. But because we didn't live in this building, the people from this building wouldn't let us in. So we had no choice. So we had to go back into this empty apartment and wait and see what's going to happen. And we sat in this empty apartment. In the meantime, we could hear the screams, and yells, and whistles on the street. And people running, and screaming, and yelling. And we just sat in this empty apartment. And to-- to-- to keep each other, boost each other morale, we used to tell jokes to each other. And as we were sitting there on the floor, there were bed bugs. And my sister wanted to kill a bed bug. And I told her, don't-- don't kill it. If you kill them, they will kill us. And we were sitting on the floor and scooting around to let the beg bugs walk around us. And all of a sudden, we hear on the stairs big sound of boots and a man's voice screaming and yelling, [GERMAN SPEECH]. Which means all Jews out, out, out. And we were just sitting there. And suddenly, the door was pushed with a butt of a rifle. And what we didn't notice is that we were sitting behind the door. We didn't notice it. The apartment was empty. The-- the-- the door flew open. We were sitting behind the door. And this man put his head into apartment. Some went in the apartment and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're sitting there I don't know for how long. And when everything was finally quiet, we walked out. And we were the only ones left in that building, because they had found the bunker. They took everybody out. And we're the only two left alive from this house. We run across the street, and my mother didn't hear what was going on, and she was safe, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, my father-- you know, this idea of the workshop with the frames went. You know, it was no longer valid. And my father registered as a wood carver. And as such, he got work in the German military cemetery, which was situated outside of the ghetto. His job was to carve the names and the ranks of the fallen German soldiers, their date of birth, and the date if death on the crosses that were then put in the cemetery to mark their graves. And as such, he was working outside of the ghetto with a pass. And he was able to bring from outside food, which was wonderful. We had again bread. Sometimes we had tomato, and potatoes, and whatever it is that he could. And with this pass, he was allowed to bring those things. And this helped us in terms of food. And one day, pass or no pass, my father was taken to Umschlagplatz. Umschlagplatz was the place where all the Jews were gathered before being transported out. My father's boss at that time was a German captain. And I wish I knew his name, but I don't. And my sister had his number, so she called him and told him what happened. And he came immediately with his car to the Umschlagplatz. And he was talking to the young SS man that was guarding the place to release my father. And my-- the young policeman said, I don't recognize your authority. He said, look at my rank. He said, I don't care. I have to have a written order from the SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was nothing that the captain could do. And he told us that tomorrow morning he's going to come with the paper. And we're crying. We're telling him, you know, tomorrow will be too late, because the transports are going on a daily basis. But there was nothing we could do. And some-- by some kind of miracle, my father succeeded to get out of Umschlagplatz and came back home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to work the following day. And he asked the captain, who was-- who had the whole battalion of Wehrmacht soldiers housed in what used to be a public school, whether he could take my mother, my sister, and me out of the ghetto to be servants to the soldiers. To do anything they need to be done. And the captain agreed, but we had to go through to the Jewish labor exchange. And when we went through the Jewish labor exchange, the labor exchange asked the captain to perhaps take more than three people. As a result, there were 30 people as Jews living in the basement of this public school, working as the-- the men as workers in the cemetery, and the women doing all kind of house duties. My sister and-- no, my sister remained in the ghetto. There was no room for her. It was only my mother and I. And I used to help my father carve the names on the crosses. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My sister stayed in the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Because she stayed in the ghetto, I had a pass to go back and forth and bring her some food. I also had an opportunity to go back to Hashomer Hatzair. What I omitted to say is that when the Warsaw uprising started, the Hashomer Hatzair was very much involved in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about before the uprising? Was Hashomer Hatzair involved in any functions of self-help or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Hashomer Hatzair was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] was a socialistic movement [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and very Zionistic. They were expert in youth education. And their whole focus was on Zionism, on Eretz Yisrael. We were taught songs. And no matter what was happening in the ghetto around us, we were kind of protected with the fantasy and the dream of living for Zionism and Eretz Yisrael. Since of course, any kind of a political activities were banned, they were not only had to cover, but they indeed have created shelters for the orphans whose parents were deported. So they had public kitchens in their headquarters, and they were taking care of all the orphans that they could that were not in the [NON-ENGLISH] place. You know, there was no room there for everybody. So they-- this is what the Hashomer Hatzair did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your role in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My role in the organization was-- we were organized as a military kind of a movement. There were battalions and-- and small groups of six, seven. And then-- the seven little groups consisted into organization of a bigger group. It was like platoons and whatever the terms are. My function at first was to teach Polish to the Jewish children who did not know how to speak Polish. How to read and write Polish. Then when the organization of the uprising started, my function was to go after curfew paste the-- the posters calling the Jews to armed rebellion on the walls of the buildings. My father, of course, was-- he didn't know what I was doing. But when I was coming home after the curfew, it was-- it was a terrible thing. They were terribly, terribly worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? How could you maneuver yourself without being caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were street wise. You know, there were patrols, but it was dark. And we knew the streets. And we're going through valley, through the backyards. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You know, we're risking our lives, but we were young. And one was going with a bucket and the brush to paste the wall, and the other one was running with the roll of posters and they are clipping them. You know, it was-- it was fun. You know, it was-- we were too young, I think, to realize how dangerous it really was. It was a kind of a sport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was adamant that the-- the order of the day from Hashomer Hatzair was that we are not going to be taken alive. You know, we are not going to be allowed to take on the transports. And I was all hyped up with it. I knew that this is exactly what I'm going to do. We are not going to go just like that. So-- there was just a conviction. We are just not going.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the others in the ghetto, the religious community or the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like in any other place, there was a great generation gap in the ghetto. The young people didn't have the responsibility of their families. They can-- they could afford to be heroic. They didn't have to worry about anybody. The older people who had families couldn't afford to be to quite that heroic. They believed that maybe by building the bunkers and all kinds of hiding places, they can survive rather than to fight. There were, of course, a Jewish council who were-- who were doing anything to appease the Germans by bribes and money. And give them money, and again money, and pay them again and again. Until one day, Czerniakow, who was then the president of the Jewish community, was asked again to give money. And there was no money. And he, in desperation, committed suicide rather then cooperate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, this was the mood in the ghetto. You know, it's-- we need to-- to think of the dynamic constellation of the ghetto not very different than the dynamic constellation of any other place when you have suddenly this little categories of the older people, rich people, poor people. And everybody protecting their own vested interests.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had heroes and you had robbers. You have smugglers on a big scale that became very rich. You had the intelligentsia that could no longer-- use their intellect that became very poor. It was a-- a confusion of values. And you had your share of heroes and robbers, and people that were taking advantage of being taken advantage of. There was a market in the ghetto where people were selling their personal possessions in exchange for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food. With the decrees that the [NON-ENGLISH] decreed against the Jews, and one of the things was that the Jews couldn't have pianos. And I remember my father selling the pianos. And a Pole came into our house, and he gave him some money and took-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and took-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no. He gave him some money, and my sister was supposed to bring him the three legs of the piano to get the rest of the money. And she did. She went on to the other side. There was one of the passages that you can still go from the ghetto to the Aryan side through the house of the-- courthouse that was on the border of the ghetto and the Aryan side. And she took the three legs, and she went to collect the money. She got the money. And as she was coming back, the people that were supposed to transport the piano were going after her. They robbed her of the money. And they told her, if you don't give us some money, we will tell-- we will give you to the SS men telling them that you are Jewish. So no legs, no money, no piano. That was just of a little incident.
